name: Typewalk 1915
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a Grotesque with roots in early 20th-century European sign painting and the proto-rational Werkbund aesthetic, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition.
summary: An eleven-weight grotesque drawing on early 20th-century European sign-painting tradition and the proto-rational language of Werkbund Objectivity, with a matching monospaced companion.
description: Designed by Sven Fuchs, Typewalk 1915 takes the quirks of pre-war European lettering culture and channels them into a versatile grotesque suitable for cultural, editorial and branding work. Eleven weights from Thin to Heavy each come with a matching italic, giving the family considerable range for print and web. Typewalk Mono 1915 extends the family into a monospaced variant, making it a coherent system across both proportional and fixed-width settings.
foundry_blurb: Typocalypse describe Typewalk 1915 as a vintage and slightly quirky Grotesque branded by history, conceived as a homage to European sign painter and lettering traditions of the early 20th century. They note it speaks in the proto-rational and graphical language of Werkbund Objectivity circa 1915, and position it as versatile for cultural, editorial and branding purposes across web and print.

name: Typewalk Mono 1915
classification_notes: Classified as mono because it uses fixed-width spacing throughout; stylistically it draws from early 20th-century European sign painting and proto-rational grotesque lettering, placing it within the grotesque mono subcategory.
summary: A fixed-width grotesque rooted in early 20th-century European sign-painting and Werkbund lettering tradition, designed by Sven Fuchs between 2012 and 2016.
description: Eight weights from Thin to Bold give the family a broad tonal range for editorial, cultural and branding work at small and large sizes. The typewriter-inflected forms reference the proto-rational graphic language associated with Werkbund Objectivity circa 1915, lending the family a period character without resorting to pastiche. A proportional companion, Typewalk 1915, is also available for contexts where fixed spacing is not required.
foundry_blurb: Typocalypse describe Typewalk Mono 1915 as a vintage typewriter grotesque branded by history, conceived as a tribute to the European sign painter and lettering tradition of the early 20th century. Designed by Sven Fuchs between 2012 and 2016 as the foundry's debut release, it speaks in what they call the proto-rational and graphical language of Werkbund Objectivity from around 1915, and they position it as versatile for cultural, editorial and branding work at small print sizes and on the web.

name: Floppy Type
designer: Kai Merker, Stefan Huebsch, Sven Fuchs
classification_notes: Floppy Type is a curated collection of twelve fonts spanning multiple styles including slab, mono and display faces, packaged as a novelty product on physical 3.5-inch floppy disk; the unifying concept is retro-computing aesthetics rather than a single typeface classification.
summary: A limited-edition bundle of twelve fonts from Typocalypse, sold on a physical 3.5-inch floppy disk alongside an instant digital download.
description: The set brings together nine distinct typefaces spanning display, slab, monospaced and icon styles, united by a retro-computing premise. Physical copies were limited to twenty floppy disks and are now sold out, though the digital download remains available. Produced by the southwest German collective Kai Merker, Stefan Huebsch and Sven Fuchs.
foundry_blurb: Typocalypse describe Floppy Type as twelve fonts delivered on a 3.5-inch limited-edition floppy disk, inviting buyers to dust off an old machine and insert it into drive A, with an instant digital download included in the meantime. The physical edition was limited to twenty copies.

name: Lichtspiele
classification_notes: Explicitly conceived as a display family evoking early 20th-century German cinema facades, with neon, marquee and dimensional variants that place it firmly in decorative/display territory.
summary: A five-subfamily display family by Stefan Hübsch evoking early 20th-century German cinema facades, with neon, outline, extruded and screen-credits variants.
description: Designed between 2012 and 2014, Lichtspiele takes its name from the German word for cinemas of the early 20th century and builds out eleven fonts across five distinct styles. Display covers a base upright with two italics offering opposing perspectives, Neon riffs on old cinema signage lettering, Neon Outline and Neon 3D add layering and extrusion, and Screen Credits draws from movie poster liner notes. The dual italic perspectives across three of the styles give the family an unusual spatial quality suited to layered poster and signage work.
foundry_blurb: Typocalypse describe Lichtspiele as transporting you back to a time of neon lights and marquee letters on cinema facades. The family spans five styles including Display, Neon, Neon Outline, Neon 3D and Screen Credits, with three of those styles offering two italic versions for different perspective effects. It was designed by Stefan Hübsch, son of a sign painter, between 2012 and 2014.

name: Black No.7
classification_notes: Inspired by a 19th-century Tennessee whiskey logo, this is an ornate display typeface with a distressed vintage variant, placing it firmly in the decorative/display category with a Western or Americana flavour.
summary: A display typeface drawn from the 1866 Jack Daniel's whiskey logo, offered in a clean Black Label version and a distressed Vintage cut.
description: Designed by Stefan Hübsch in 2009, Black No.7 takes its cue from the famous Tennessee whiskey logo of 1866. The family ships in two variants: a clean Black Label and a distressed Vintage version, with over 400 glyphs covering ligatures, alternates and special characters. It suits packaging, branding and editorial work where a heritage Americana aesthetic is called for.
foundry_blurb: Typocalypse describe Black No.7 as inspired by the famous Tennessee whiskey logo from 1866, offering over 400 glyphs including ligatures, alternates and special characters. The foundry note that the font should not be used commercially with the associated logos without first contacting the relevant companies for official guidelines and artwork.
